chapters.title: Knowing the root of stalling helps you handle it
chapters.text:
Most of us have wrestled with putting things off at some point. Yet we tend to write it off as a lapse in judgment instead of digging into why we do it in the first place. In the chapters ahead, you will see why this habit wrecks your life and how to climb out of that hole.

Putting things off is often confused with laziness, yet a staller is different. A lazy person never gets things done. A staller eventually does the task, but only after a long fight.

Positive qualities like prudence, patience, and prioritizing share one thing with stalling. They all involve delay. Yet they are not the same as putting things off. Stalling is far more than pushing tasks to a later time. It is irrationally postponing work even when you know it could harm you.

When we face the fallout of stalling, we promise ourselves it will never happen again. The problem with that promise is that it fails to treat the habit as something ingrained. So we fall into self-deception because we cannot wish the behavior away or, worse, justify it away.

Research shows that impulsiveness sits at the heart of stalling.

Our craving to live in the now pushes us to put off what we should be doing. The craving cuts across every field of human effort and is slightly stronger in men than women.

For those of us who are less impulsive, worry often acts as an internal cue that gets us started on a project early. For those who are more impulsive, the story is different. Worry over a deadline leads straight to stalling, as Piers Steel has written.

When a person is impulsive, they avoid any task that brings worry. Even if they start, they get pulled away by something else in no time.

Should you be doing something yet cannot find the motivation to start. This summary will help you understand what stalling is and what to do about it. You will see the formula and learn how to apply it.

For context, the word procrastination first showed up in English in the 16th century.

chapters.title: The formula: expectancy times value, divided by impulsiveness times delay
chapters.text:
The formula has four parts: Expectancy, Value, Impulsiveness, and Delay.

Expectancy is the confidence you have in your ability to create the thing you are trying to create. Value describes the meaning you attach to the work. Impulsiveness covers the things likely to pull you away from doing the task. Delay deals with how close the deadline sits. The further away the deadline, the less likely you are to do the task.

These factors decide whether we stall or not, and they stand apart from the strongest social science theories of motivation. We tend to delay tasks that are not urgent, and that growing delay drains our motivation. When impulsiveness combines with this fuzzy sense of how much time we actually have, stalling becomes inevitable. The only counterweight is the value we place on the task and our confidence in the payoff. For example, if you promise a child video games after finishing their chores, they will speed through the chores, but only if they value the games and trust that you will keep your word. They are less likely to get pulled away and they will not delay, because they know finishing fast means more play time.

To curb stalling, we have to push expectancy and value up while pulling impulsiveness and delay down.

For every task, ask yourself these questions. How sure am I that I can pull it off. That is Expectancy. Does this work have real meaning to me. That is Value. How likely am I to be pulled away by instant gratification. That is Impulsiveness. How long will this project take to finish. That is Delay.

Answering these questions reveals the strategies you need to put in place to finish on time. It also helps you see whether this is a task you should be doing at all.

chapters.title: Building a success spiral to raise your confidence
chapters.text:
A success spiral is a strategy that helps maximize motivation toward a goal. It works by breaking the main goal into challenging but achievable milestones. As you cross each milestone, you build the momentum you need to reach the next one until the whole goal is done. Each milestone must feel meaningful and show you what you are capable of. Polynesian explorers colonized the South Pacific using spirals. When they spotted a new island from their home port, they made it their next achievable goal. They focused on reaching it and built plans to get there. After settling it, they turned their attention to the next island and made it their new target.

Each win propels us toward the next win.

To raise expectancy, we need to create success spirals. It does not have to be a new island in the South Pacific. It can be as simple as finishing your first task of the day. Making your bed in the morning, for example, can propel you to get more done. If you do not believe you can pull something off, you may not want to spend the energy trying. And if you have not finished something recently, how do you know you can pull off a new goal. You do not have any momentum.

The opposite of the success spiral is the downward spiral. Stalling can pull you into it. You keep piling up unfinished projects and lose faith in your ability to finish new ones. The good news is that the ability to spiral down also means you can spiral up. Think of dominoes. Knocking over the first domino uses the power of compounding to knock down the next ones, and you only need to focus on that first domino.

Plan your day the night before. Decide what you will finish first the next day. Begin your day by doing something meaningful before other activities crowd in. Make a checklist and cross items off as you finish them. Reminding yourself of your wins helps you build momentum.

A good day eventually spirals up into a good life.

chapters.title: Raising the worth of a task through mental contrasting
chapters.text:
Visualization needs mental contrasting to help you beat stalling in a useful way. When you paint mental pictures of where you want to be, you have to compare them with where you are now, and that comparison adds worth to your goals. Creating visuals of a goal without this second step drains motivational fuel.

Mental contrasting does not create optimism, but it maximizes the motivational benefits of optimism, creating energy, effort, and a jumpstart in planning, as Piers Steel has written.

If you have been practicing creative visualization, you do not have to stop. You only need to add a picture of where you are right now. The following steps help. Fix an image of the life you want in your mind. Pick one aspect of that life and focus on it. Articulate why this piece of your future life is attractive to you. Mentally contrast that life with where you are at the moment. Vividly describe the gap between your current life and your visualized life.

Taking these steps will motivate you to pursue the ideal life you have painted.

Value is a key part of the formula.

Raising the value or expected value of what you want helps you find the strength to act now.

The acronym WOOP describes a practice that combines the vision of a compelling future with reality so that the precise action becomes clear. WOOP stands for Wish, Outcome, Obstacles, and Plan. Wish asks what important wish you want to pull off. Outcome asks what the best result would be from achieving that wish. Obstacles asks what the main obstacle is in reaching this goal. Plan asks what is an effective action to tackle that obstacle.

Wish is the first step and is like visualization. Outcome is the second step and describes the value of the wish. Obstacles focuses on the gap between your reality and the wish. Plan names the practical steps you will take when the obstacles show up.

chapters.title: Cutting distractions gives you an extra month each year
chapters.text:
Distractions are everywhere and they take more than they give. Removing them lets you focus on one task and see it through. For example, turning off audio notifications in your email settings raises efficiency by 10 percent, according to Piers Steel. Over a year, that adds up to one extra month.

Check your email at a set time rather than every time you hear the beep.

Do not underestimate the role distraction plays in getting things done. It takes extra time to get back into full focus after a break. And every time you allow yourself to be pulled away from work, you have to summon the strength to resume.

This practice helps you cut impulsiveness. Yet it is not enough to raise expectation and value. You also have to pull impulsiveness and delay down.

While cutting distractions is a key step toward lowering impulsiveness, it is often hard to disable every notification on your devices. Put your phone on airplane mode and in another room when you are doing your best work, and shut down browser extensions that pull you to distracting sites. You will be tempted to stop doing this, and it is important to push through those temptations.

Step into flow and become ten times more productive. Flow is a mental state where you can get ten times the amount of work done in the same span of time. You can create and recreate the flow state if you set up your environment correctly. Beyond removing distractions, pick a specific goal and a specific time frame during which you will work without interruptions. Use focusing music to boost concentration on the work in front of you.

chapters.title: Skimping on sleep fuels stalling because you are too tired to work
chapters.text:
Sleep matters because it makes nearly everything better. We set alarms to wake up, yet sometimes we need to set alarms to go to sleep, since eight hours of sleep each night improves productivity during the day. Plenty of sleep boosts cognitive performance, so tasks stop feeling cumbersome.

Tiredness sits at the top of the list of reasons people put things off. Sleep helps beat tiredness.

Set up a sleep routine. Pick a specific time when you need to be in bed and build a pre-bed ritual that calms your nervous system, like drinking tea, reading a book, or meditating. Going straight from work to bed can make it hard to fall asleep. Also, create a good sleep environment by turning off the lights, using dark curtains, or playing slow music.

Goal setting, the kind that is done properly, is the smartest move you can make to fight stalling, as Piers Steel has written.

Whenever we are stressed, we lose willpower, and when willpower wanes, we give in to impulses instead of steering them. So we end up doing things we should not and putting off the ones we should. We have to recognize that there is a limit to the fuel we have in a day. That recognition lets us budget the fuel we have and refuel when needed. For example, a dieter should not wait until they are starving before eating, or they will fall for the carbs and fats all around them. Similarly, to fight stalling, use your moments of strength to remove the things that easily distract or drain you.

Study yourself to know whether you are sharpest early in the morning or a night owl. Schedule your most important tasks a few hours after you wake up, since that is when you are likely to be most efficient. A short nap during the day can extend your efficient phase. If you cannot nap, take a walk at lunchtime.

For reference, the liver metabolizes alcohol best about 12 hours after we wake up.

chapters.title: Closing thought
chapters.text:
Set challenging and meaningful goals to maximize motivation and beat stalling by using the strategies shared here. Set your goals right after this closing, so you do not put off applying the solutions.

Be as specific as you can when setting goals and pick an actual deadline. If the goal is long-term, apply positive spirals by breaking it into smaller milestones. If a goal feels hard to reach, find a portion of it you can tackle. Reaching that milestone helps you cut the tendency to delay. For example, you could decide to write a single page of a book you have been putting off. Writing that page may be all the momentum you need to write a whole chapter. And writing a chapter may propel you to write more chapters.

Create a routine around your goals and stick to the schedule. Pick a time when you do that specific task and a place to do it.

The formula demands that we raise expectation and value while lowering impulsiveness and delay. So make your goals specific and time-based by creating a plan with actionable steps. Then name the daily activities you must do to follow your plan.

Finally, since screens are the number one way we stall, we need to band together to build self-control into these devices. For example, if these devices recorded and reported our activities, we might be able to curb the excesses. What we measure can be improved. So stalling can be defeated if we all band together to insist on controlling our devices rather than letting them control us.

Try this. Whatever you are putting off, take the following steps. Write down what you want to finish and when, and be specific. Break the goal into short-term milestones or objectives. Create daily activities you have to do to reach the milestones.

Read it till the end.
